ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for pea shoots as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for this vegetable subtype is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw pea shoots is approximately 50 g (about 1 cup loosely packed), commonly used as a salad base or lightly blanched side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Pea shoots, raw — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for pea shoots, raw (F14010029). Sodium recorded as -1 in source, indicating data not available; treated as not reported rather than zero.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw pea shoots found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 15 based on analogous non-starchy leafy green vegetables (e.g., spinach, lettuce) with very low carbohydrate content (1.8 g/100 g). Glycemic load per 50 g serving = (15 × 1.8 × 0.50) / 100 = 0.14, rounded to 0.1. This is consistent with the extremely low carbohydrate density of the food.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high potassium (380 mg/100 g) with sodium data not reported (treated as negligible); potassium-to-sodium ratio strongly favourable for blood pressure management.
hypertension.guidance: Pea shoots are an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals due to their very high potassium content and negligible sodium; consume raw or lightly blanched without added salt or high-sodium sauces to preserve the blood-pressure benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely low carbohydrate (1.8 g/100 g), low sugar (0.39 g/100 g), high protein (4.2 g/100 g), and 2.0 g dietary fibre per 100 g produce a negligible glycaemic impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pea shoots are highly suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management; their very low carbohydrate and sugar content combined with meaningful protein and fibre minimise postprandial glucose excursions, and they can be consumed freely as a salad or lightly cooked side dish without sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (0.7 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium (380 mg/100 g), and meaningful dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g) support a cardioprotective dietary pattern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pea shoots are cardioprotective due to their very low fat and cholesterol content, high potassium, and fibre; include regularly as a raw or lightly cooked vegetable and avoid preparation with saturated-fat-rich oils or high-sodium sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pea shoots provide calcium (49 mg/100 g) and iron (1.3 mg/100 g) which support bone and haematological health in RA, but lack confirmed omega-3 fatty acids or established anti-inflammatory polyphenols sufficient to meet RA suitability criteria.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pea shoots may be included in an RA diet for their micronutrient content (calcium, iron, potassium) but should not be relied upon as a primary anti-inflammatory food; consume raw or lightly cooked and avoid high-sodium stir-fry sauces that can worsen tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pea shoots (豌豆苗, dou miao) are the young tender shoots, leaves, and tendrils of the pea plant (Pisum sativum), harvested before the plant matures. They are widely consumed across Chinese and Southeast Asian cuisines, particularly in Singapore, Hong Kong, and southern China, where they are prized as a premium leafy green. Raw pea shoots have a bright, vivid green colour, a crisp yet delicate texture, and a mild, sweet, slightly grassy flavour reminiscent of fresh peas. They are commonly eaten raw in salads, lightly blanched, or quickly stir-fried with garlic and minimal seasoning to preserve their delicate character. Nutritionally, pea shoots are exceptionally nutrient-dense relative to their very low caloric content of 30 kcal per 100 g. They provide 4.2 g protein, 1.8 g carbohydrate, 0.7 g fat, and 2.0 g dietary fibre per 100 g, making them one of the highest-protein leafy vegetables available. Their potassium content of 380 mg/100 g is notably high, supporting cardiovascular and blood pressure health. Calcium (49 mg/100 g) and iron (1.3 mg/100 g) contribute to bone and haematological health. The glycaemic index is estimated to be very low (approximately 15), consistent with other non-starchy leafy greens, making pea shoots an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and are suitable for low-calorie, low-carbohydrate, and ketogenic dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: Are pea shoots good for diabetes?
Can I eat pea shoots if I have high blood pressure?
How to eat pea shoots raw

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14010029), an approved primary regional source, with exact match to the food item. Sodium is recorded as -1 in the source, indicating the value was not reported rather than measured as zero. GI is an analogous estimate from approved GI literature given the absence of a direct measurement for this specific food.
notes: sodium_mg is set to -1: the HPB Singapore Foodo source (F14010029) does not report a sodium value for raw pea shoots; the original input sentinel value of -1 is preserved. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, and moisture data for raw pea shoots are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14010029) as of 2026. saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0: confirmed negligible per HPB source for this low-fat raw leafy vegetable (total fat 0.7 g/100 g). cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — pea shoots are a plant food and contain no cholesterol. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy leafy green vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for raw pea shoots was found in approved sources. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis listed suitable: false but the reason text described positive nutritional attributes; this has been reconciled in health_context as moderate concern with nuanced clinical guidance. Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible best-match assignments; SNOMED CT code not identified for this food subtype.
